content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • )100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660        322-5901        322-6441        322-6444

November 4, 1980

Vigo G. Nielsen, Jr.
Dobbs & Nielsen
The Alcoa Building, Suite 2500
1 Maritime Plaza
San Francisco, California 94111

A-80-11-115

Dear Mr. Nielsen:

Thank you for your letter dated October 27, 1980 regarding the
provision of food and beverage to state officials at a conference
held by the Institute of Governmental Advocates.

The facts, as you relate them, are as follows:

The Institute of Governmental Advocates, a trade association
whose membership consists primarily of lobbyists, collects
funds from its members and pays overhead costs plus costs
associated with two conventions/legislative seminars held
each year. In addition to its regular business on matters
relevant to registered lobbyists, the association has hosted
legislators, constitutional officers and administrative
officials who participate on panels and/or as featured
speakers. For the Institute's up-coming Winter Conference
a legislative seminar is planned and it is contemplated that
a number of legislators will be invited to a morning panel
to briefly discuss the next legislative session and allow
the new freshmen to explain their goals. The association
will host the legislative participants to cocktails and
lunch, but will not be providing any travel or accommodation
reimbursement.

You asked for confirmation that the costs of the cocktails and lunch
are neither payments or exchanges within the provisions of FPPC
Regulation 18623(a), even though the association's membership is
funded by lobbyists.

2 Cal. Admin. Code Section 18623(a) states:

"As used in this section, 'honorarium' means a payment for
speaking at any event, participating in a panel or seminar
or engaging in any similar activity. For purposes of this

/86223

Vigo Nielsen                          -2-                          November 4, 1980


     section, free admission, food, beverages and similar nominal
benefits provided to a filer at an event at which he or she
speaks, participates in a panel or seminar or performs a
similar service, and a reimbursement or advance for actual
intrastate travel and for necessary accommodations provided
directly in connection with the event are neither payments
nor exchange."

     Accordingly, the provision of cocktails and lunch to the legislators
as described in your letter is an honorarium and not a payment or
exchange in accordance with 18623(a) because:

     1) the Winter Conference is a regular semi-annual convention/
legislative seminar of the Institute of Governmental Advocates,
and as such is the type of "event" covered in 2 Cal. Admin. Code
Section 18623(a);

     2) the legislative seminar together with the proposed panel
is part of the agenda for the Winter Conference; and

     3) the luncheon is directly in connection with the Winter
Conference.

     I hope this advice has been helpful. If I can be of further assistance,
please do not hesitate to contact me.

                                        Cordially,

                                        [illegible]
                                        Rich Eichman
                                        Accounting Specialist

DOBBS & NIELSEN
Attorneys and Counselors at Law

SUITE 2800, THE ALCOA BUILDING
ONE MARITIME PLAZA
SAN FRANCISCO, CA 94111

TELEPHONE (415) 362-1940

October 27, 1980


J. Richard Eichman
Compliance Representative
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA  95814

Re:  Clarification of FPPC Reg. 18623

Dear Rich:

     This office represents the Institute of Governmental
Advocates, a trade association whose membership consists
primarily of lobbyists registered pursuant to the Political
Reform Act of 1974, as amended.

     This Association collects funds from its members and
pays overhead costs plus costs associated with two Conventions/
Legislative Seminars held each year.  Besides attending to its
regular business and receiving educational and legal reports
on matters relevant to registered lobbyists, the Association
has hosted legislators, constitutional officers and administra-
tive officials who participate on panels and/or as featured
speakers.

     For this Winter Conference a Legislative Seminar is
planned where it is contemplated that a number of legislators
will be invited to a morning panel to briefly discuss the up-
coming session and allow the new freshmen to explain their
goals.

     The Association will host the participants to cocktails
and lunch, but will not be providing any travel or accommodation
reimbursement.


SACRAMENTO OFFICE - SUITE 550, 1225 EIGHTH STREET, SACRAMENTO, CA 95814  (916) 446-6752
LOS ANGELES OFFICE - SUITE 309, 612 SOUTH FLOWER STREET, LOS ANGELES, CA 90017  (213) 622-5867
NEWPORT BEACH OFFICE - SUITE 4000, 4000 MACARTHUR BOULEVARD, NEWPORT BEACH, CA 92660  (714) 752-9318

J. Richard Eichman
October 27, 1980
Page 2

It is our belief that such an event fits within the
provisions of FPPC Regulation 18623(a), even though the
Association's membership is funded by lobbyists and, there-
fore, we have not attributed any of the costs to individual
lobbyists since, according to 18623, these costs are "neither
payments nor exchanges".

We would appreciate your clarification of our interpre-
tation of this Regulation so that we can proceed with extending
invitations.

Very truly yours,

Vigo G. Nielsen, Jr.

VGN:dc

cc: Robert T. Monagan, President
    Institute of Governmental Advocates
